facts.fact: A cold TLS 1.2 handshake requires 3 total round-trips (1 for TCP three-way handshake + 2 for TLS negotiation), translating to 600-750ms for a user in Asia connecting to a US server with 200-250ms RTT
TLS 1.3 reduces the TLS portion to 1 RTT (2 RTTs total), saving 200-250ms on intercontinental connections
TLS session resumption with 0-RTT allows a client to include encrypted application data in the first packet, eliminating handshake latency for repeat connections
Layer 4 load balancers make routing decisions based solely on IPs and TCP/UDP ports without inspecting message content
Layer 7 load balancers terminate network traffic, decrypt as needed, inspect messages, and make content-based routing decisions on HTTP headers, URLs, and cookie data
For gRPC deployments using multiplexed HTTP/2, Layer 4 load balancing can cause uneven load distribution because it routes entire connections to a single backend, not individual requests
Google recommends Cache-Control max-age values longer than 6 months or a year for static resources when using cache-busting patterns with version hashes in URLs
Static assets should use 'public' and 'immutable' directives in Cache-Control headers to enable caching by both end-user browsers and intermediate CDN proxies
Dynamic content like HTML and API responses should use 'no-cache' or low TTL values with 'private' to ensure quick delivery of personalized experiences without serving stale data
Omitting a Cache-Control header allows browsers to heuristically cache responses without explicit control, leading to unpredictable caching behavior
Time To First Byte (TTFB) should be 0.8 seconds or less; values greater than 1.8 seconds are considered poor
Network transmission (request + response over the network) accounts for approximately 40% of TTFB
Dynamic content generation (PHP, database queries) is the most common culprit for high TTFB, with large files and slow queries as primary contributors
Cache stampede (dogpile effect) occurs when a popular cached item expires under heavy load, causing multiple concurrent requests to regenerate it simultaneously from the origin
With cache stampede, if a cached page takes 3 seconds to render and has 10 requests per second traffic, cache expiry can trigger 30 simultaneous render operations
CDNs implement request collapsing at the edge: when multiple simultaneous requests hit a stale entry, only one forwards to the origin while others are held and served from the result
Cache invalidation by tag allows purging all cached content associated with a tag; Cloudflare marks tagged content as stale while revalidation happens in the background
A good CDN cache hit ratio (CHR) should range between 95-99%, meaning less than 5% of requests go to origin
Cloudflare CDN caching can reduce origin load by up to 70% and deliver sub-50ms response times globally
A WordPress site normally taking 900ms can deliver content in under 35ms from Cloudflare's edge—a 25x improvement
Brotli compression produces approximately 20% smaller files than gzip for text-based content
Netflix Open Connect achieves approximately 98% cache hit rate, with origin traffic being only 2% of edge traffic
Netflix's two-tier CDN architecture uses Storage Appliances at internet exchange points holding nearly the entire catalog, and Edge Appliances within ISPs caching regional content
Origin Shield is an additional caching layer between CDN edge servers and the origin, reducing load on the origin by aggregating requests from all regional edge caches
HTTP/2 multiplexing allows multiple requests to be sent in parallel over a single connection, eliminating head-of-line blocking present in HTTP/1.1
HTTP/2 with 256 concurrent streams achieved 115,000 requests per second compared to 6,500 for HTTP/1.1
An e-commerce site's HTTP/1.1 had 3-second page loads under high concurrency due to head-of-line blocking; switching to HTTP/2 reduced it to 1.5 seconds
Persistent HTTP/2 connections reduce DNS lookups by approximately 33% compared to establishing new connections per request
Invalidating a CDN cache has no effect on browsers or ISP caches; once cached at the user's browser or ISP level, the content cannot be remotely removed
TLS 1.3 with PSK (Pre-Shared Key) resumption replaces legacy session IDs, enabling 0-RTT resume connections with a single round trip
0-RTT TLS 1.3 resumption can lead to replay attacks since early data lacks state information; mitigated by regular session key rotation

examples.description: An e-commerce site announces a flash sale at noon. At 12:00:00, millions of users hit the product page simultaneously. If the product cache expired at 11:59:55, a stampede of millions of database queries crashes the system.
examples.lesson: Cache hit rates directly protect origin infrastructure from capacity collapse during traffic spikes

examples.description: A weather website caches city data with a 5-minute TTL. When cached weather for a major city expires, 50,000 simultaneous user requests arrive asking for that city's weather.
examples.lesson: High-traffic content requires either long TTLs, stale-while-revalidate patterns, or request collapsing to prevent stampedes

examples.description: A news aggregator caches article metadata with a 2-minute TTL. When a major story breaks, traffic spikes 100x. Every 2-minute expiry becomes a mini-stampede that cascades upstream.
examples.lesson: Time-based cache expiration doesn't scale; event-based invalidation (tag-based purge) is better for dynamic content

examples.description: Netflix pre-positions encoded video segments to edge appliances within ISP networks during off-peak hours. During peak viewing hours, users fetch manifests and segments from the nearest appliance with 98% cache hit rate, requiring only 2% origin requests.
examples.lesson: Strategic content prepositioning and edge caching reduce origin traffic by 50x and improve global latency

examples.description: Fastly uses Shielding to reduce origin load during cache misses: all requests from edge POPs route through a single regional shield node before reaching origin, creating a secondary cache that catches request batches.
examples.lesson: Multi-tier caching (edge → shield → origin) multiplies cache hit ratios and protects origin from thundering herd

examples.description: A company uses multiple CDNs (Cloudflare + CloudFront) simultaneously for redundancy. Origin Shield aggregates requests from all CDN edge servers, preventing the origin from seeing 10x request amplification.
examples.lesson: Origin Shield amortizes origin capacity across multiple CDN providers, preventing over-provisioning

examples.description: A Kubernetes cluster runs gRPC services on 10 pods. With Layer 4 load balancing, all traffic from a single client lands on pod #1 while pods #2-10 sit idle. Switching to Layer 7 balancing distributes individual gRPC calls evenly across all 10 pods.
examples.lesson: Multiplexed protocols (HTTP/2, gRPC) require Layer 7 load balancing; Layer 4 treats entire connections as indivisible

examples.scenario: TLS Handshake Latency in Asia-US Connection
examples.description: A user in Mumbai (250ms RTT to US East) establishes a fresh HTTPS connection. TLS 1.2 adds 500ms of handshake overhead (2 RTT). Upgrading to TLS 1.3 reduces it to 250ms (1 RTT). Enabling session resumption with 0-RTT eliminates it entirely for repeat visits.
examples.lesson: Protocol version and session management compound: TLS 1.3 + resumption saves 500ms per new connection

analogies.explanation: Origin is the publisher. Regional bookstores (edge POPs) hold popular titles. Origin Shield is a central warehouse between bookstores and publisher. Customers (users) buy from the nearest bookstore first; if a title is out of stock, the bookstore orders from the warehouse, not the publisher.

analogies.analogy: Cache stampede as a bank run
analogies.explanation: When a cached resource expires, it's like rumors of a bank closure. All depositors (requests) rush to withdraw simultaneously. The bank (origin) collapses under the rush. Request collapsing is like a queue: one person withdraws cash, the teller photos it, and gives copies to the line behind them.

analogies.analogy: Layer 4 vs Layer 7 load balancing as mail routing
analogies.explanation: Layer 4 looks at the destination address and sender zip code, routing all mail from one postal district to one delivery center. Layer 7 reads the contents of each letter and routes divorce filings to the courthouse, tax returns to the IRS, and love letters to the romance section.

A cold TLS handshake is like two people meeting in person: handshake, exchange information, agree on rules—three back-and-forths take time. Session resumption is like meeting an old friend: you recognize them immediately (0-RTT) and skip the introduction.

public/private is like marking books as open-access (shared) vs. reference-only (personal). max-age is the book's return date. no-cache is 'ask the librarian if the version is current.' immutable is 'this book never changes; don't check the catalog again.'

analogies.analogy: HTTP/2 multiplexing as airport boarding lanes
analogies.explanation: HTTP/1.1 is a single boarding lane that processes one passenger at a time, sequentially. Even fast passengers are blocked by slow ones. HTTP/2 opens multiple lanes on a single jetway: passengers board in parallel without interfering. gRPC is one long-lived boarding session where individual passengers flow through multiple lanes simultaneously.

analogies.analogy: Origin Shield as a wholesale warehouse
analogies.explanation: Edge POPs are retail stores. Without Origin Shield, each store orders inventory independently from the factory, creating redundant orders. Origin Shield is a wholesale warehouse: stores order from the warehouse, which consolidates requests and orders once from the factory, saving bandwidth and origin capacity.

key_numbers.context: Reduces TLS negotiation from 2 RTT to 1 RTT; TCP still requires 1 RTT

key_numbers.value: 0ms for TLS; still requires TCP SYN/ACK (~1 RTT)
key_numbers.context: TLS 1.3 with session tickets or PSK; replay vulnerability requires mitigation
Measured from navigation start to first byte of response
User-perceptible slowness; commonly caused by database queries or large asset generation
The remaining 60% is server processing + queueing
Means 1-5% of requests reach origin; enables 10-50x origin capacity reduction

key_numbers.metric: Netflix Open Connect cache hit rate
key_numbers.context: Only 2% of edge traffic reaches origin; achieved through strategic prepositioning
Served from nearest regional POP; 25x faster than uncached origin
With aggressive caching; combination of cache hit ratio and compression
For text-based content; cost: 20-40% higher CPU on compression
Compared to 6,500 req/s for HTTP/1.1; ~17x improvement
E-commerce site under high concurrent load; head-of-line blocking eliminated

key_numbers.metric: DNS lookup reduction with persistent connections
key_numbers.context: HTTP/2 persistent connections reuse DNS; reduces latency per request

key_numbers.value: 30 concurrent renders when 1 expires under 10 req/s + 3s render time
key_numbers.context: Origins typically run 8-16 processes; 30 simultaneous jobs oversubscribe CPU
Invalidates cached content in near-real-time via tag-based purge

key_numbers.value: Layer 4: ~1-5ms decision time; Layer 7: ~20-50ms (content inspection + routing)
key_numbers.context: Trade-off: Layer 4 speed vs Layer 7 intelligence

key_numbers.metric: Origin Shield cache hit ratio boost
key_numbers.context: Depends on traffic distribution; higher for bursty workloads

common_misconceptions.misconception: Cache invalidation removes content from all caches globally
common_misconceptions.reality: Cache invalidation only affects CDN edge caches. Browser caches and ISP caches cannot be remotely invalidated. Once cached at the user's browser or ISP, the content persists until the browser's TTL expires or the user manually clears cache.
While HTTP specs say POST should invalidate caches, real-world implementations (Firefox, some CDNs) don't follow the standard correctly. POST is not a reliable cache invalidation mechanism.

common_misconceptions.misconception: A high cache hit ratio (95%+) means the CDN is performing well
common_misconceptions.reality: Most CDNs count any response from their cache (even stale content) as a 'hit.' This metric can be misleading; a CDN might count stale served-with-revalidate as a hit while origin gets pounded. TTFB and origin load are better indicators.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Layer 4 load balancing works fine for gRPC
common_misconceptions.reality: gRPC multiplexes many requests over a single long-lived TCP connection. Layer 4 load balancers see the connection, not individual requests, so all traffic can land on one backend. Layer 7 is required for gRPC load distribution.

common_misconceptions.misconception: TLS session resumption is always secure
common_misconceptions.reality: TLS 1.3 0-RTT resumption with PSK does not provide forward secrecy and is vulnerable to replay attacks. Mitigated by regular key rotation and anti-replay mechanisms, but not 'free security.'

common_misconceptions.misconception: Omitting Cache-Control headers means content isn't cached
common_misconceptions.reality: Without explicit Cache-Control headers, browsers use heuristic caching rules, caching content indefinitely. This is dangerous; always set explicit Cache-Control to control caching behavior.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Higher max-age always means better performance
common_misconceptions.reality: Long max-age on dynamic content causes stale content to persist. Short max-age + must-revalidate is often faster for dynamic content because revalidation (304 Not Modified) is cheaper than regeneration.

TTFB = redirect time + DNS + TCP + TLS + server processing. Network transmission is ~40%; server-side processing is the majority. Improving TTFB requires both network optimization and origin optimization.

common_misconceptions.misconception: CDN caching eliminates the need for origin optimization
common_misconceptions.reality: Cache hits reduce origin load, but cache misses still hit origin at full speed. Origins must be performant because cache misses are inevitable (new content, expired items, bypass policies).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Layer 4 load balancing is always faster than Layer 7
common_misconceptions.reality: Layer 4 has lower latency (~1-5ms vs 20-50ms), but Layer 7 can make smarter routing decisions. For high-throughput scenarios where latency matters more than distribution quality, Layer 4 wins. For application-aware routing, Layer 7 is required.
